text.title: Cross-Role Handoff Protocol — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every handoff must enforce. Designed to eliminate the most common multi-week-flow failure: "I thought you signed off" with no record.

rule.statement: Every handoff has EXACTLY ONE named sign-off owner on the downstream side. Group sign-off ("the QA team approved") is forbidden — pick the individual who is accountable.
rule.rationale: Group accountability decays to nobody-accountable. When a defect escapes, "the team approved" produces no learning. A single name forces the owner to actually read the bundle and lets retros target a specific decision.

rule.statement: Each sign-off record MUST include the timestamp + the artifact-delta-since-previous-sign-off. If the upstream role re-submitted after rejection, the record references the diff between the rejected submission and the accepted one.

rationale.source: Toyota A3 / hansei practice, postmortem literature

rule.rationale: Without the delta, "approved Tuesday" tells the retro nothing. The delta is what makes the gate auditable — it shows WHAT changed to clear the gate. Postmortems that lack this field cannot identify which gate failed.

rule.statement: Downstream role may NOT start work before the upstream gate is signed off. "Parallel work to save time" is forbidden — it converts handoff debt into rework. The only exception: explicit expedite path, which requires a project-manager sign-off with documented risk acceptance.

rationale.source: Project management body of knowledge, 'Shape Up' expedite-path discussion

rule.rationale: Parallel-start is the #1 source of mid-sprint rework. The expedite exception exists because real emergencies exist; making it explicit + signed prevents creeping normalization of skipping.

rule.statement: A "no" sign-off MUST cite at least one specific missing artifact OR one specific failing acceptance-gate item. Generic "not ready" rejections are forbidden — they create handoff ping-pong with no learning.
rule.rationale: Vague rejection cycles waste 2-3 handoff iterations on clarification. Forcing the reason field makes the upstream role's remediation deterministic.

rule.statement: Once a sign-off record is created, it MUST NOT be edited. Corrections happen via a new sign-off record that explicitly references the prior one (status=reversed or status=amended). The audit trail is append-only.

rationale.source: Audit / compliance practice, ITIL change records

rule.rationale: Editable sign-offs break audit trails. If QA signed off Monday and the feature broke Wednesday, the team must be able to see EXACTLY what QA saw Monday — not a quietly-edited version.

example.title: Architect->Dev handoff with delta
example.code: Handoff: feat/billing-overhaul, Architect -> Dev Sign-off owner: alex.k (named Dev lead) Timestamp: 2026-05-14T16:42:00Z Status: approved Gate items passed: - ADR-2026-014 attached - API contract OpenAPI spec attached + lint passes - Migration plan attached (3-phase, dual-write window 7d) - Cost-latency budget signed by architect lead Delta since previous submission (2026-05-13 rejected): - Added missing dual-write rollback procedure - Added per-tenant feature flag plan Reason for prior rejection: missing rollback procedure (specific item)
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r4, r5: single named owner, delta logged, prior rejection reason concrete, record append-only.

example.title: Verbal handoff with no record
example.code:
Slack message from Architect to #eng: "spec is ready, devs can start" Result: - 3 devs started; one used the v1 spec, one used the v2 from doc-edit history, one waited for clarity. - QA could not say which spec the build represents. - Retro outcome: "we need better handoffs" -> no specific change.
Repair: enforce protocol. The Slack message is fine as a NOTIFICATION, but it does not replace the handoff-record artifact attached to the feature ticket. Without the record, no parallel work begins.
example.note: Illustrates r1, r3, r5 violations: no named sign-off, parallel-start without gate, no audit trail.
